title: Present Perfect — Actions with Present Relevance
description: Use 'have/has + past participle' to connect past experiences and recent events to the present moment.

items.back: I have seen / She has gone
items.example: I have visited Paris twice. He has just left.

items.back: at any time / not at any time
items.example: Have you ever been to Japan? I've never tried sushi.
I've already finished the report — it wasn't that hard!
Have you eaten yet? — No, not yet.
She's just arrived. The coffee is still hot.
I've lived here for five years / since 2019.
I haven't seen her recently. Have you spoken to him lately?
Have you done your homework? I've never eaten durian.

items.back: I have / He has / They have
items.example: She's lived abroad for two years. We've known each other forever.

items.prompt: I ___ that film three times — it's my favourite!
items.explanation: Unspecified number of times with present relevance → present perfect: have seen.

items.prompt: Have you eaten ___? — No, not yet. I'm still waiting for my order.
items.explanation: 'Yet' is used in questions and negatives for things expected to happen. 'Not yet' = no todavía.

items.prompt: She ___ just ___ from her trip to Colombia. She looks so happy!
items.explanation: She has just returned = she returned very recently. 'She's just returned.'

items.prompt: I've known Marco ___ we were children. We grew up in the same street.
items.explanation: 'Since' introduces the starting point of an ongoing state: since we were children.
'Ever' in questions = at any time in your life. 'Have you ever…?' asks about experience.

items.sentence: I ___ never ___ sushi. Is it good? (eat)
She ___ already ___ the report — she's very efficient. (finish)
I've lived in this city ___ ten years. I love it here.
Have you ___ your homework yet? The deadline is tomorrow.
He ___ just sent the email. Check your inbox now.
